policy_statement: Ban outdoor work from 11:00 AM to 16:00 PM during June through August under a national law issued in 2005 (occupational heat protection).
mechanism_description: Restricting outdoor work during peak-heat hours reduces workers’ exposure to extreme heat and associated health risks.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (extreme heat) → ↓ exposure (avoid peak-heat work) → ↓ heat strain/illness and related harm
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Work schedules and/or legal permissions for outdoor work are modified (e.g., shifting hours, mandating midday work bans in hot months). (2) Mediating process: Reduced time spent outdoors during peak wet-bulb/air temperatures lowers heat load on the body (less radiant/convective heat gain and often lower dehydration risk). (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced heat-related morbidity/mortality and improved occupational safety under increasing extreme heat frequency/intensity.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced human health risk by lowering workers’ exposure to extreme heat during the hottest hours/days.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: addresses the climate hazard (extreme heat) by reducing exposure through time-of-day/season restrictions on outdoor work.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: the mechanism’s intent and main effect is preventing heat impacts on people (health and safety), not changing energy/material use (Resource Efficiency), reducing GHGs (Mitigation), or enhancing ecosystems (Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Inherent secondary chains: none are guaranteed—any emissions/energy changes (e.g., more night lighting or cooling, altered productivity) are context-specific and not an unavoidable physical consequence of restricting work hours.  
4) Edge cases applied: even if some implementations indirectly change energy demand or commute patterns, those effects vary by sector and enforcement and do not define the mechanism.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: any policy that restricts outdoor work during peak heat functions primarily as heat-risk reduction via exposure avoidance, fitting Adaptation universally.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential non-inherent side effects include shifted energy use (lighting/night work, cooling), economic/productivity impacts, or reduced ozone exposure from less midday outdoor activity; these are implementation- and context-dependent and therefore not classified as secondary categories.
key_indicators: “issued a national law”; “stop any outdoor work”; “from 11:00 AM to 16:00 PM”; “during the months of June through August”
co_benefits: Potential productivity protection, reduced dehydration-related incidents, reduced burden on health services, improved general occupational safety (not specified as explicit objectives/mechanisms in the text).
instance_edge_case_notes: No explicit mention of enforcement/penalties or heat-index thresholds; classification relies on the clear legal prohibition of outdoor work during peak summer hours.
